slides.title: Stop Repeating Yourself
slides.body: A **for loop** lets Python do repetitive work for you — visit every item in a collection and do something with it. Write the action once; Python runs it for every item.
slides.tip: For loops are one of the most-used tools in all of Python.

slides.title: Your First Loop in Action
slides.body: The pattern is `for item in collection:` — Python hands you one item at a time, runs your indented code, then moves to the next.
slides.tip: Name your loop variable descriptively — `fruit` not `x` — so the code reads like English.

slides.title: Count Numbers with range()
slides.body: Use `range()` when you want to loop over a sequence of numbers. `range(5)` gives you `0, 1, 2, 3, 4` — it stops **before** the number you pass in.
slides.tip: range(start, stop) — the stop value is always excluded. Want 1–5? Write range(1, 6).

slides.title: Build Results as You Loop
slides.body: Start with a zero or an empty list **before** the loop, then add to it on each pass. This **accumulator** pattern unlocks huge power.
slides.code:
numbers = [3, 7, 2, 9, 4]
total = 0
for num in numbers:
    total = total + num
print("Sum:", total)
slides.tip: Set up your accumulator before the loop starts, not inside it.

slides.title: Index + Value with enumerate()
slides.body: Need to know **where** an item is in the list as well as what it is? `enumerate()` gives you both — no manual counter needed.
slides.tip: enumerate() is cleaner and more Pythonic than range(len(my_list)).

slides.title: You've Got the Loop Locked In!
slides.body: You now know how to loop over **lists**, **strings**, **ranges**, and even grab **indexes** with `enumerate()`. The for loop is your single most powerful everyday Python tool — use it everywhere.
slides.tip: When you have a known collection to work through, a for loop is almost always the right choice.
